skills.title: Making Assumptions
skills.note: Protects Section 1 & 3. Listen to context clues, speaker relationships, and environmental noises to assume who is speaking and where.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Listen for nouns, terms, or vocabulary that reveal the setting (e.g., *balcony, flat, deposit, rent* indicates a real estate context).
  • Analyze the speakers' tone and address styles (e.g., *Professor, doctor, classmate*) to assume their social relationship.

items.q: Speaker: 'We have garage parking spaces available for a low monthly fee... and storage areas in the basement.' What is the speaker's job?
items.a: Identify context: rent, parking, basement storage. Infer role: The speaker is an apartment house manager or leasing agent.

skills.title: Understanding Numbers
skills.note: Avoids Common Typos. Distinguish between 'teen' (-teen) and 'ty' (-ty) pronunciations, and follow zip codes/phone numbers.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Pay close attention to syllable stress: numbers ending in *-teen* have stress on the last syllable (e.g., fif-TEEN); numbers ending in *-ty* have stress on the first syllable (e.g., FIF-ty).

items.q: Audio: 'Our office number is seven-two-one, double one-two-seven.' Write down the phone number.
items.a: Listen to chunk 1: 'seven-two-one' -> 721. Listen to chunk 2: 'double one' -> 11. Listen to chunk 3: 'two-seven' -> 27. Number: 721-1127.

skills.title: Understanding the Alphabet
skills.note: For Spelling Tasks. Follow spelling sequences closely, noting double letters, homophones, or silent letters.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Identify common spelling sounds: 'A' vs 'E' vs 'I', 'G' vs 'J', 'Y' vs 'U'.

items.q: Audio: 'My name is Kingston. James Kingston. That's K-I-N-G-S-T-O-N.' Write down the spelling.
items.a: Listen to ejaan: K - I - N - G - S - T - O - N. Combine: Kingston.

skills.title: Distinguishing Similar Sounds
skills.note: Aids Transcription Accuracy. Practice listening for minimal pairs like 'fifteen'/'fifty' or word-ending consonants.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Practice minimal pairs—words that differ in only one sound, such as *bad* vs *bed*.

items.q: Audio: 'The room is fifty square meters.' Question: 'How big is the room? 15 or 50?'
items.a: Listen to syllable stress: 'FIF-ty' has stress at the start. Compare context: '15' would be 'fif-TEEN'. Answer: 50.

skills.title: Listening for Descriptions
skills.note: For Diagram Labeling. Follow adjectives describing shape, size, location, and materials to match labels.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Listen for relative direction words (*across from, next to, behind, opposite, on the left-hand side*).

items.q: Audio: 'On the corner of my street there is a grocery store. Across from that, there is a dry cleaner. Next to the dry cleaner is a clothing store.' Where is the clothing store?
items.a: Trace landmark 1: Grocery store is on the corner. Trace landmark 2: Dry cleaner is across from grocery store. Trace clothing store: Next to dry cleaner (so it is also across the street from the grocery store).

skills.title: Listening for Time
skills.note: Avoids Scheduling Errors. Track dates, specific hours, duration, and time-zone indicators (*a.m./p.m.*).
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Listen for time changes or updates: speakers often correct themselves.

items.q: Audio: 'The first bus leaves the station at 6:45 a.m., but that is the express bus. The local bus leaves 30 minutes later.' When does the local bus leave?
items.a: Base time: Express bus at 6:45 a.m. Add duration: Local bus is 30 minutes later. Calculate: 6:45 + 30 mins = 7:15 a.m. Answer: 7:15 a.m.

skills.title: Listening for Frequency
skills.note: For Habit-Based Questions. Differentiate adverbs of frequency (*always, twice a week, annually*) from one-off events.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Listen for how often an activity occurs. Look for words like *every day, bi-weekly, once a year*.

items.q: Audio: 'I try to visit the library every day to study, but with my busy class schedule, I only get there twice a week.' How often does the speaker visit the library?
items.a: Analyze: Speaker wants to go every day (intent). Extract reality: 'only get there twice a week'. Answer: Twice a week / Two times a week.

skills.title: Listening for Similar Meanings
skills.note: Overcomes Paraphrases. Differentiate synonyms in statements to match the speaker's true intent.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Questions use different words than the audio. Listen for synonyms of the question keywords.

items.q: Question: 'Is the exercise room free for members?' Audio: 'Is it included in the rent? No, it’s available for a small extra fee.' Answer the question.
items.a: Listen to terms: 'small extra fee'. Compare synonym: 'free' would mean no fee. Answer: No (there is an extra fee).

skills.title: Listening for Emotions
skills.note: For Attitude/Opinion Questions. Listen to tone of voice, sighs, pauses, or pitch to determine how the speaker feels.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Listen to the speaker's tone: flat tones suggest boredom/indifference, rising pitches suggest excitement.

items.q: Audio: (Sighs) 'Even though I was nervous when I first walked into the examination room, the examiner put me at ease.' How did the speaker feel initially?
items.a: Listen to initial emotion: 'nervous'. Identify change: 'put me at ease' (relieved). Initial feeling: Nervous / Anxious.

skills.title: Listening for an Explanation
skills.note: For Flowcharts & Processes. Follow links of cause-and-effect or steps in a process layout.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Listen for process markers (*first step, then, after that, finally*).

items.q: Audio: 'As a result of this increase in blood flow, the system is unable to function effectively.' What is the cause of the system failure?
items.a: Identify connector: 'As a result of...'. Extract the cause following the connector: 'increase in blood flow'. Answer: Increase in blood flow / Blood flow increase.

skills.title: Listening for Classifications
skills.note: For Table-Completion Questions. Listen to details that categorize concepts into distinct groups.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Track categories during the lecture (e.g., Group A vs Group B).

items.q: Audio: 'Dormitories are for full-time students. Apartments, on the other hand, are best suited for married couples.' Classify apartments.
items.a: Categorize dorms: Full-time students. Categorize apartments: Married couples. Classification of apartments: Best suited for married couples.

skills.title: Listening for Comparisons and Contrasts
skills.note: Aids Multiple-Choice. Distinguish comparative structures (*whereas, like, unlike, different from*).
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Listen for contrasting words: *but, however, on the other hand, whereas*.

items.q: Audio: 'The university campus has many older brick buildings, whereas the new library is a modern concrete structure.' Compare the campus and library.
items.a: Identify campus buildings: Older, brick. Identify library: Modern, concrete. Contrast: Campus buildings are old/brick; library is new/concrete.

skills.title: Listening for Negative Meaning
skills.note: For T/F/NG or MC. Listen for negation prefixes (*un-, in-, dis-*) or restrictive adverbs (*hardly, except*).
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Negation is not always just 'not'. It can be prefixes like *disagree, misunderstand*.

items.q: Audio: 'All students are allowed to park in the garage, except for freshmen.' Can freshmen park in the garage?
items.a: Listen to restriction: 'except for freshmen'. Apply rule: Freshmen are excluded. Answer: No (freshmen are not allowed).

skills.title: Listening for Chronology
skills.note: Follows Timelines. Track time sequence markers (*initially, subsequently, finally*) to order lists.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Listen for chronological transitions to avoid mixing up the order.

items.q: Audio: 'Initially, the land was used for farming. Subsequently, a local businessman bought it and built an airport.' Order the uses.
items.a: Initial use: Farming. Subsequent use: Airport. Chronological order: 1. Farming -> 2. Airport.

audioscripts.title: Part 1 Dialogue: Apartment Rental & Client Intake
audioscripts.transcript:
Agent: Good morning, elite real estate solutions. How may I assist you today?
Client: Yes, hello. I was calling to inquire if you have any one-bedroom flats available in the downtown sector?
Agent: We certainly do. I have two spacious apartments facing the central park. May I take down your full name first?
Client: Yes, it is James Kingston. I will spell that for you: K-I-N-G-S-T-O-N.
Agent: Thank you, James. And what is your primary contact number?
Client: My home phone is 721-0584, but I am usually at the office. You can reach me there at 721-1127. That is double one-two-seven at the end.
Agent: Got it. Do you require parking or basement storage?
Client: Yes, I definitely need a secure garage space for my car, and a lockable storage cage in the basement.
audioscripts.notes: Spelling Key: K-I-N-G-S-T-O-N. Key Numbers: Work phone is 721-1127. Renter requests a garage parking space and basement storage.

audioscripts.title: Part 2 Monologue: Campus Library Orientation Tour
audioscripts.transcript: Guide: Welcome to the university library orientation. The library is closed every Wednesday morning for staff training, but open at all other times. On the corner of our entry street, you will notice the student grocery store. Directly facing it, across from the grocery store, is our printing and binding office. Next to the printing office is the library's main clothing repository and locker area where you must deposit all bags. For a low monthly fee of five pounds, you can also rent a private research cubicle on the third floor.
audioscripts.notes: Relative Directions: Printing office is across from grocery store. Locker area is next to the printing office. Schedule: Closed Wednesday mornings.

audioscripts.title: Part 3 Group Discussion: Final Thesis Project Outline
audioscripts.transcript:
Professor: So, Sarah, how is your final biology project coming along? You look a bit nervous.
Sarah: (Sighs) Well, I was extremely anxious when I began reading the guidelines last week. But after discussing it with Tom, I feel much more relieved. My study compares the behavior of crows and chimps.
Professor: Excellent. Remember, chimps are highly social, whereas crows are more solitary tool-users. Your project must classify the behaviors into three categories: tool manufacturing, communication signals, and nesting styles.
audioscripts.notes: Emotions: Initially anxious, now relieved. Contrast: Chimps are social whereas crows are solitary. Classification categories: Tool manufacturing, communication signals, and nesting styles.

audioscripts.title: Part 4 Lecture: The Evolution of Hunter-Gatherer Societies
audioscripts.transcript: Lecturer: Good afternoon. Today we examine early social structures. Initially, humans survived as nomadic bands, traveling constantly to follow migrating herds. Subsequently, with the discovery of grain storage, they established sedentary settlements. As a result of this sedentary transition, populations grew rapidly, leading to the rise of specialized labor and social hierarchies that replaced the original egalitarian system.
audioscripts.notes: Chronology Order: Nomadic bands -> Grain storage discovery -> Sedentary settlements. Cause-and-Effect: Sedentary transition caused population growth, leading to hierarchies.

vocabulary.example: This townhouse has a large bedroom and a small den, ideal for set up a desk.
You must pay a security deposit equivalent to one month's rent before moving in.
Since the flat lacks laundry hookups, I use the local laundromat down the block.
Nomadic tribes migrated constantly to seek fresh water and pasture.
Developing agriculture allowed humans to abandon a nomadic life for a sedentary one.
The professor handed out the course syllabus detailing the weekly readings.
The orientation lecture will be held in the main university auditorium at 9:00 a.m.
Any hesitation or long pause in your speech will lower your fluency band score.
Varying your vocal pitch helps convey enthusiasm and emotion clearly.
The registration desk is located on the ground floor of the administration building.
